paragraph_or_text_span: NICE guideline Reference number: NG91 Published: 28 March 2018 Last updated: 11 March 2022 Download guidance (PDF)
1.1 Managing acute otitis media 1.2 Choice of treatment

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that: acute otitis media is a self-limiting infection that mainly affects children acute otitis media can be caused by viruses and bacteria, and it is difficult to distinguish between these (both are often present at the same time) symptoms last for about 3 days, but can last for up to 1 week most children and young people get better within 3 days without antibiotics complications such as

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
paragraph_or_text_span: Assess and manage children under 5 who present with fever as outlined in the NICE guideline on fever in under 5s . [2018]

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
paragraph_or_text_span: Give advice about the usual course of acute otitis media (about 3 days, can be up to 1 week). [2018]

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer regular doses of paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain. Use the right dose for the age or weight of the child at the right time, and use maximum doses for severe pain. [2018]

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider eardrops containing an anaesthetic and an analgesic for pain (see recommendation 1.2.1 for choice of treatment ) if: an immediate oral antibiotic prescription is not given (see recommendations 1.1.8 to 1.1.14 ), and there is no eardrum perforation or otorrhoea. Review treatment if symptoms do not improve within 7 days or worsen at any time. [2022]

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
paragraph_or_text_span: Explain that evidence suggests decongestants and antihistamines do not help symptoms. [2018]

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
paragraph_or_text_span: Reassess at any time if symptoms worsen rapidly or significantly, taking account of: alternative diagnoses, such as otitis media with effusion (glue ear) any symptoms or signs suggesting a more serious illness or condition previous antibiotic use, which may lead to resistant organisms. [2018] For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice

heading_path: Children and young people who may be less likely to benefit from antibiotics (those not covered by recommendations 1.1.11 to 1.1.14)
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider no antibiotic prescription or a back-up antibiotic prescription (see recommendation 1.2.1 for choice of treatment ), taking account of: evidence that antibiotics make little difference to symptoms (no improvement in pain at 24 hours, and after that the number of children improving is similar to the number with adverse effects) evidence that antibiotics make little difference to the develo

heading_path: Children and young people who may be less likely to benefit from antibiotics (those not covered by recommendations 1.1.11 to 1.1.14)
paragraph_or_text_span: When no antibiotic prescription is given, give advice about: an antibiotic not being needed seeking medical help if symptoms worsen rapidly or significantly, do not start to improve after 3 days, or the child or young person becomes systemically very unwell. [2018]

heading_path: Children and young people who may be less likely to benefit from antibiotics (those not covered by recommendations 1.1.11 to 1.1.14)
paragraph_or_text_span: When a back-up antibiotic prescription is given, give advice about: an antibiotic not being needed immediately using the back-up prescription if symptoms do not start to improve within 3 days or if they worsen rapidly or significantly at any time seeking medical help if symptoms worsen rapidly or significantly, or the child or young person becomes systemically very unwell. [2018] For a short expla

heading_path: Children and young people who may be more likely to benefit from antibiotics (those of any age with otorrhoea or those under 2 years with infection in both ears)
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider no antibiotic prescription with advice (see recommendation 1.1.9), a back-up antibiotic prescription with advice (see recommendation 1.1.10) or an immediate antibiotic prescription (see recommendation 1.2.1 for choice of treatment ), taking account of: evidence that acute complications such as mastoiditis are rare with or without antibiotics possible adverse effects of antibiotics, partic

heading_path: Children and young people who may be more likely to benefit from antibiotics (those of any age with otorrhoea or those under 2 years with infection in both ears)
paragraph_or_text_span: When an immediate antibiotic prescription is given, give advice about seeking medical help if symptoms worsen rapidly or significantly, or the child or young person becomes systemically very unwell. [2018] For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the evidence and committee discussion on no antibiotic , back-up antibiotics and c

heading_path: Children and young people who are systemically very unwell, have symptoms and signs of a more serious illness or condition, or are at high risk of complications
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer an immediate antibiotic prescription (see recommendation 1.2.1 for choice of treatment ) with advice (see recommendation 1.1.12). [2018]

heading_path: Children and young people who are systemically very unwell, have symptoms and signs of a more serious illness or condition, or are at high risk of complications
paragraph_or_text_span: Refer children and young people to hospital if they have acute otitis media associated with: a severe systemic infection (see the NICE guidelines on suspected sepsis in under 16s and suspected sepsis in people aged 16 or over ) acute complications, including mastoiditis, meningitis, intracranial abscess, sinus thrombosis or facial nerve paralysis. [2018] For a short explanation of why the committe
